key_claim: Wheelchair accessibility in autonomous vehicles must be prioritized to improve equitable transportation access for people with disabilities in rural and low-income communities.
methodology: Consumer and provider perspective study examining design considerations for accessible autonomous transportation services
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer or grassroots coordination mechanisms among underserved populations to fill transportation gaps before AV deployment
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Demand management policies can improve ride bundling and system performance in rural shared mobility-on-demand services despite unfavorable spatial and temporal demand distributions.
methodology: Development of a methodology evaluating different demand control policies applied to an operational planning model for rural shared mobility-on-demand systems
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, trust mechanisms, or grassroots user self-organization in coordinating shared rides in rural settings
theoretical_framework: Operational demand management framework with demand control policies for shared mobility-on-demand

key_claim: Governance of multimodal integration in rural areas depends on network maturity and scope, with barriers of ambiguous responsibility and interoperability gaps between transport modes.
methodology: Comparative case study of two Dutch rural mobility hubs (Groningen-Drenthe and Zeeland) using an institutional network-of-networks analytical lens
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how end-users or student populations self-organize grassroots coordination to bridge gaps left by institutional multimodal governance failures
theoretical_framework: Network governance framework combining institutional network-of-networks analysis for multimodal transport integration

key_claim: Electric autonomous ride-sharing services offer a promising but currently limited alternative to enhance VA transportation access for veterans in rural and underserved areas.
methodology: Qualitative interviews with eight VA transportation stakeholders representing seven transportation sectors
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-driven platform design primitives such as identity verification, rating fairness, or gamification to improve coordination among transportation users
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Institutional design parameters governing multimodal integration determine whether rural areas achieve sufficient public transport modal availability.
methodology: Comparative case-study analysis of three Dutch regions using a design framework developed from literature review and expert workshops (n=16), comparing six institutional parameters across public transport, paratransit, and shared mobility.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how international students or university populations in small towns self-organize informal multimodal coordination outside formal institutional designs.
theoretical_framework: Six-parameter institutional design framework for intermodal comparison (initiative, decision-making level, service requirements, network size, risk allocation, and additional parameter)

key_claim: Small autonomous shuttles best suit flexible non-routine trips in rural areas, with leisure travelers and university precincts as the most important operational determinants.
methodology: Structured survey of 273 residents in rural and peri-urban South-East Queensland, analyzed using General Additive Model (GAM) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) to identify operational determinants.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer or grassroots ride-sharing coordination among students as a complementary or alternative solution to autonomous transit in underserved areas.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Best-worst scaling importance-performance analysis reveals which public transport attributes university authorities should prioritize to improve campus commuting services.
methodology: Best-worst scaling experimental design with importance-performance analysis (IPA) estimated via a Mixed Logit model, surveying university students commuting to campuses in Gran Canaria.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address student-initiated informal transportation solutions such as carpooling platforms or how identity-verified peer coordination supplements formal public transit to campus.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of university students in Gran Canaria
theoretical_framework: Importance-Performance Analysis (IPA) framework combined with Best-Worst Scaling and Mixed Logit estimation

key_claim: Personal photos serve as trust signals on Airbnb, influencing reputation formation and booking decisions in the sharing economy.
methodology: Not fully determinable from available metadata; likely empirical analysis of Airbnb listing data examining the role of personal photos on trust and reputation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification and personal photo-based trust mechanisms translate to non-commercial peer-to-peer platforms such as campus carpooling.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Digital reputation systems on platforms like Airbnb embed power asymmetries that undermine their reliability as fair communicators of trustworthy market behavior.
methodology: Normative ethical analysis of digital reputation systems (DRSs) examining trust, reliability, and fairness in platform economies
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification mechanisms interact with reputation scores to mitigate power asymmetries in peer-to-peer platforms
theoretical_framework: Trust and power analysis of Digital Reputation Systems (DRSs)

key_claim: Professionalizing e-hospitality sharing platforms along ticket-clipper and end-to-end dimensions can contribute to sustainable sector growth in local communities.
methodology: Hierarchical linear modeling using Airdna vacation rental data for HomeAway/Stayz with a conceptual narrative distinguishing two professionalism dimensions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots coordination or safety mechanisms for users in peer-to-peer mobility or multi-module campus platforms
theoretical_framework: Ticket-clipper vs. end-to-end professionalism framework for sharing economy platforms

key_claim: Implicit rather than explicit host self-disclosure on Airbnb leads to more guest trust, contrary to expectations.
methodology: Online experimental survey with two conditions (implicit vs. explicit host self-disclosure) comparing 129 Dutch and 71 Chinese participants
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification or platform-enforced trust mechanisms interact with self-disclosure to build trust beyond hospitality contexts
theoretical_framework: Self-disclosure theory applied to trust and booking intention in sharing economy platforms

key_claim: Three types of online trust-building mechanisms differentially affect providers' trust in the sharing platform versus trust in consumers.
methodology: Structural equation modeling with partial least squares (PLS-SEM) based on a survey of 209 providers on online short-term rental platforms
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification as a distinct trust-building mechanism or how rating fairness and gamification affect trust in non-hospitality peer-to-peer platforms
theoretical_framework: Online trust-building mechanisms framework distinguishing platform trust from consumer trust

key_claim: Perceived risk heightened by COVID-19 negatively moderates the relationship between trust and usage intention on Airbnb and Uber platforms.
methodology: Quantitative survey comparing Airbnb and Uber users, testing structural equation models of trust antecedents and usage intention moderated by COVID-19 perceived risk.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address trust dynamics in grassroots, identity-verified peer-to-peer platforms outside commercial sharing economy giants, nor small-town or university contexts.
theoretical_framework: Trust transfer theory with structural assurance, privacy protection, social influence as antecedents; perceived risk as moderator

key_claim: Customer-related, platform-related, and service-provider-related factors all significantly drive customer participation on sharing platforms across 192 independent samples.
methodology: Meta-analysis of 192 independent samples from 167 studies involving 171,344 customers, synthesizing effect sizes of antecedents of sharing platform participation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address participation dynamics in non-commercial, grassroots coordination platforms or identity-verified campus-specific services where trust mechanisms differ from mainstream platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: Meta-analytic dataset of 167 studies / 192 independent samples / 171,344 customers
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Trust is the major factor in peer-to-peer accommodation decision-making, and existing research relies on diverse theoretical models to understand Airbnb and Couchsurfing.
methodology: Umbrella-type literature review of secondary sources on Airbnb and Couchsurfing, cataloguing concepts and theoretical models used by prior scholars.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address trust-building in identity-verified, multi-module platforms for university communities or non-accommodation peer-to-peer services like carpooling.
theoretical_framework: Disruptive innovation framework applied to P2P accommodation / network hospitality

key_claim: White guests largely overcame racial bias in Airbnb host selection when hosts were endorsed by previous white guests, showing same-race endorsements offset rather than reinforce discrimination.
methodology: Observational analysis of Airbnb booking and review data examining in-group preference for same-race hosts and same-race recommendations, with tests for affective enthusiasm in endorsement content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification and rating fairness mechanisms in small-scale, grassroots platforms could proactively mitigate racial bias rather than relying on organic endorsement patterns.
theoretical_framework: In-group preference / social identity theory applied to peer endorsement and racial bias in platform markets

key_claim: Corporate digital responsibility of sharing economy platforms can be structured into value, balance, and accountability categories using a refined VBA framework.
methodology: Conceptual extension of the VBA framework using integrative top-down and bottom-up approaches with Airbnb and Uber as illustrative cases
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots user-driven coordination mechanisms or identity-verification design primitives for safety in sharing platforms
theoretical_framework: Value-Balance-Accountability (VBA) framework

key_claim: Attitude significantly mediates the effects of effort expectancy, social influence, and facilitating conditions on Indian consumers' intention to use Airbnb.
methodology: Survey-based quantitative study (N=301) using an extended meta-UTAUT model with hedonic motivation, trust, and self-efficacy
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address multi-module platform coordination challenges in resource-constrained settings such as small-town university environments
theoretical_framework: Meta-UTAUT (Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology)

key_claim: Romanian tourists' motivations for choosing Airbnb are shaped by trust, digital skills, and perceived benefits of the collaborative platform.
methodology: Cross-sectional quantitative survey with non-random convenience sampling among Romanian tourists
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address platform design primitives such as identity verification or safety mechanisms beyond general trust and digital skills
dataset_or_benchmark: Convenience sample survey of Romanian tourists
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Specific trust-influencing factors significantly affect travelers' behavioral intention to use Airbnb across the Philippines, Indonesia, and Singapore.
methodology: Mixed-methods study using self-administered questionnaires (N=130) supplemented by informal interviews, analyzed with Hayes' Process Macro
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity-verified trust mechanisms or multi-module coordination platforms beyond commercial accommodation sharing
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 130 Airbnb users from the Philippines, Indonesia, and Singapore
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Consumer loyalty toward Airbnb is shaped by both dedication-based and constraint-based mechanisms, with affective and calculative commitment playing distinct roles.
methodology: Structural equation modeling (SEM) on survey data from 156 Airbnb consumers who used the platform more than twice.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address loyalty mechanisms in non-commercial, identity-verified community platforms or grassroots coordination settings such as small-town university carpooling.
theoretical_framework: Dedication-based and constraint-based loyalty framework (affective and calculative commitment)

key_claim: Hybrid business models in the sharing economy integrate access, platform, and community-based economy elements to create balanced strategies strengthening overall operations.
methodology: Integrative literature review and synthesis of studies on hybrid business models and the sharing economy, advancing a conceptual framework.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how hybrid business models apply to grassroots, student-organized multi-module platforms or identity-verified safety mechanisms in small-town university contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Literature corpus on hybrid business models and the sharing economy
theoretical_framework: Conceptual framework for hybrid business models encompassing value proposition, governance, coordination, resource allocation, sustainability, reputation, and ecosystem partners

key_claim: All six e-servicescape subdimensions positively influence consumer perception of P2P hospitality platforms, while demographic characteristics show no significant impact.
methodology: Questionnaire-based survey of 135 P2P platform users in hospitality, analyzed via reliability analysis and hypothesis testing in SPSSv31.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address e-servicescape design in non-hospitality sharing platforms such as carpooling, nor the role of identity verification or safety-specific design primitives.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 135 P2P hospitality platform users
theoretical_framework: E-servicescape framework (aesthetics, social presence, perceived personalization, perceived trust and safety, perceived interactivity, superior functionality)

key_claim: A dual-framework (ADO and TCM) review of 92 studies reveals that sharing economy user decisions are shaped by economic, social, technological, trust-related, environmental, and political antecedents.
methodology: Systematic literature review of 92 studies using Antecedents–Decisions–Outcomes (ADO) and Theories–Contexts–Methods (TCM) dual-framework analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots multi-module coordination platforms in small-town university settings or specific design primitives like identity verification, rating fairness, and gamification.
theoretical_framework: ADO (Antecedents–Decisions–Outcomes) and TCM (Theories–Contexts–Methods) dual-framework

key_claim: Algorithm-based telematics scoring can supplement peer ratings to improve trust-building in peer-to-peer carsharing platforms.
methodology: Design case study with 16 problem-centered interviews, co-design of an app with scored profiles, and evaluation with 12 participants.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification as a trust mechanism or how algorithmic scoring interacts with safety concerns specific to multi-module campus platforms.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Trust-building mechanisms in P2P carpooling platforms have differential effects depending on car ownership and user experience level.
methodology: Mixed-method study combining qualitative comparison of trust-building mechanisms across four carpooling platforms with a laboratory experiment (N=163).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification as a trust primitive or trust dynamics in small-town university settings where user populations are constrained.
theoretical_framework: Framework distinguishing online trust, implicit trust, and reason-based trust in carpooling platforms

key_claim: Platform workers appropriate private instant messaging chat groups to restore mutualism, build communities of practice, and organize resistance not afforded by official delivery apps.
methodology: Multi-country qualitative study using participant observation in five countries, 68 in-depth interviews with food delivery couriers, and digital ethnography within dozens of private chat groups.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots self-organization via messaging apps could be formalized into platform design primitives such as identity verification or gamification.
theoretical_framework: Affordances-in-practice (Costa 2018)

key_claim: Community WeChat groups with active participation from both community officials and core residents significantly improve community risk communication satisfaction during a pandemic.
methodology: Online survey of 1006 residents across 350 Shanghai communities with multiple linear regression analysis examining participant roles, perceived communication quality, and dissemination themes.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address non-crisis grassroots coordination needs such as multi-module campus services or how messaging-group governance translates to platform design for international student populations.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: WhatsApp groups enable ultra-Orthodox Jews to negotiate contested identities, crystallizing a new social identity through communal affinity, practiced agency, and contesting dogmatism.
methodology: Content analysis of 70 public Haredi WhatsApp groups and 40 semi-structured interviews with participants
what_it_does_NOT_address: How identity negotiation in secluded messaging spaces translates into practical coordination behaviors such as ride-sharing or resource pooling among minority communities
theoretical_framework: Third spaces of identity negotiation in online social networks

key_claim: WhatsApp has potential to bridge formal and informal learning spaces for university faculty and students in Namibia and India.
methodology: Survey-based study of 182 university faculty and students across Namibia and India examining WhatsApp use for learning support
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether WhatsApp-based coordination extends beyond learning to multi-module student services such as transportation, housing, or safety in university settings
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 182 participants from universities in Namibia and India
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Spontaneous self-organized volunteer initiatives in Israel grow through technology-enabled information transfer and foster community belonging and perceived effectiveness.
methodology: Case study using participant observation and semi-structured interviews with spontaneous self-organized volunteers in Israel
what_it_does_NOT_address: How spontaneous grassroots volunteer coordination can be formalized into sustained platform-mediated services such as carpooling or multi-module coordination for specific populations like international students
dataset_or_benchmark: Participant observation and semi-structured interviews (Israel crisis volunteering case study)
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Residents' WeChat group use positively impacts pro-community behavior through the mediating roles of community trust and community attachment during COVID-19.
methodology: Online survey of 500 commercial housing community residents in Wuhan, China, analyzed using structural equation modeling with SPSS 26.0 and Mplus 8.3
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether group-chat-mediated community trust and attachment translate into coordinated multi-module service provision such as identity-verified ride-sharing or resource sharing among transient populations like international students
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 500 residents in Wuhan commercial housing communities
theoretical_framework: Mediation model linking WeChat group use to pro-community behavior via community trust and community attachment

key_claim: Mobile Instant Messaging platforms like WhatsApp are used in boundary-crossing informal learning settings for knowledge creation, sharing, supervision, and collaborative problem solving in global health.
methodology: Survey with open- and closed-ended questions administered to 40 respondents across 44 project settings in global health contexts
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how MIM platforms could support multi-module coordination for international students in small-town university settings beyond health contexts
dataset_or_benchmark: Custom survey of 40 respondents across 44 global health project settings
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: International students are more fearful of crime in the United States than in their home countries, and media consumption cultivates this fear consistent with cultivation theory.
methodology: Structural equation modeling (SEM) on a survey sample of 398 international students across nine U.S. universities
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how platform-mediated safety features like identity verification or rating systems could mitigate international students' fear of crime in everyday coordination activities such as carpooling
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 398 international students at 9 U.S. universities
theoretical_framework: Cultivation theory

key_claim: Social media as a digital marketing tool significantly shapes Chinese students' perceptions, expectations, and decision-making processes regarding studying in the United States.
methodology: Model analysis approach using the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) to identify influential factors in study-abroad decisions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how social media or digital platforms support international students' post-arrival coordination needs such as transportation, housing, or community building in small-town settings
theoretical_framework: Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT)

key_claim: Korean female doctoral students in the U.S. wrestle with feelings of shame tied to embodied knowledge of race and racism, disrupting racial stereotypes and Asian invisibility in racial discourses.
methodology: Collaborative autoethnography with arts-based methods including digital collage and poetry, drawing on Critical Race Theory and Asian Critical Theory
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots platform coordination or practical multi-module tools could alleviate systemic challenges international students face beyond racialized discourse
dataset_or_benchmark: Autoethnographic narratives of 3 Korean female doctoral students
theoretical_framework: Critical Race Theory (CRT) and Asian Critical Theory (AsianCrit)

key_claim: Chinese international students' parents employ four coping strategies—open communication, social media learning, respectful sensitivity, and encouraging help-seeking—when discussing depression with their children abroad.
methodology: In-depth interviews with 13 Chinese parents of CIS, analyzed using constant comparative method
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots peer-coordination platforms or university-provided digital tools could supplement parental coping strategies for international student mental health.
dataset_or_benchmark: 13 in-depth interviews with Chinese parents of CIS
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: A one-touch SOS Android app transmitting real-time GPS location to pre-registered contacts via SMS can enhance women's personal safety in emergency situations.
methodology: Design and development of an Android mobile application with real-time location tracking, emergency alerts, fake call system, and SOS functionality; effectiveness evaluation based on user feedback
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification of contacts or riders, multi-module coordination across transportation and safety domains, or trust mechanisms relevant to shared mobility platforms.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Integrating real-time GPS tracking, crime heatmap visualization, Dijkstra-based safe routing with crime weightage, and an AI chatbot into a single web application can comprehensively address women's safety threats.
methodology: Web application development using React.js, Node.js, Firebase, Socket.IO; features include GPS tracking, automatic audio recording, crime heatmaps, safe routing via Dijkstra's algorithm, license plate verification, and Google Gemini API-powered chatbot
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity-verified peer-to-peer coordination (e.g., carpooling), rating fairness, or gamification mechanisms that build sustained community trust among users.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Personal safety apps are shifting from manual panic-button models to context-aware, sensor-fused, AI-assisted systems integrated with police or commercial monitoring centers, yet significant technological gaps remain.
methodology: Systematic review of eight major SOS/personal-safety apps synthesizing 20+ peer-reviewed studies, industry reports, and government evaluations (2020–2025)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots peer-coordinated platforms such as identity-verified carpooling, multi-module student coordination, or trust mechanisms like rating fairness and gamification in small-town university contexts.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: User perceptions and perceived quality of public transportation significantly shape transport mode choices in the Dominican Republic.
methodology: Nationwide survey of 1254 inhabitants proportional to national census demographics, analyzing perceptions, usage, and perceived quality of public transportation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots coordination or platform-based interventions could supplement public transit gaps in underserved or small-town settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: Nationwide survey of 1254 Dominican Republic inhabitants (ONE census-proportional)
theoretical_framework: Desired quality paradigm for public transportation

key_claim: Games and gamification show promising potential to impact multiple climate change engagement dimensions simultaneously.
methodology: Systematic literature review of 64 research outputs comprising 56 different gamified approaches for climate change engagement.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how gamification can be applied to platform-mediated coordination tasks such as carpooling or multi-module campus services rather than climate engagement.
dataset_or_benchmark: Corpus of 64 research outputs (56 gamified approaches)
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Gamification promotes environmentally responsible behavior through psychological need satisfaction and belief/attitude persuasion in the postadoption stage.
methodology: Survey-based study (N=1869) with a model integrating persuasion and motivation theories and the affordances–psychological outcomes–behavioral outcomes framework, applied to Ant Forest users.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether gamification mechanisms effective for environmental behavior transfer to safety-critical coordination contexts like identity-verified carpooling.
theoretical_framework: Affordances–psychological outcomes–behavioral outcomes framework combined with persuasion and motivation theories

key_claim: The convergence of machine learning and gamification is primarily applied in education, personalization, behavioral change, and context adaptation.
methodology: Systematic literature review investigating the intersection of gamification and machine learning techniques across multiple application domains.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how ML-enhanced gamification could be applied to multi-module platform coordination or rating fairness in peer-to-peer services like carpooling.
dataset_or_benchmark: Systematic review corpus (number of papers not specified in abstract)
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Walking-based and intra-team gamified challenges significantly enhanced user engagement and improved sustainable mobility behaviors among high school participants.
methodology: 13-week field intervention using GPS-based behavioral tracking and self-report data, analyzed by HEXAD Player Types, Big Five traits, and user experience evaluations within a gamified mobile app (Play&Go).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, safety mechanisms, or coordination challenges specific to multi-module campus platforms in small-town university settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: High School Challenge 2024 campaign data (Lecco, Italy)
theoretical_framework: HEXAD Player Types and Big Five personality traits as moderators of gamification effectiveness

key_claim: Eco Drive demonstrates that integrating GPS tracking, user input, and gamification in a mobile app can encourage individuals to reduce travel-related carbon emissions.
methodology: Design and implementation of a Java-based mobile application integrating GPS tracking, user input, and gamification features for carbon footprint tracking.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address multi-module coordination (e.g., carpooling, housing, errands), identity verification, or safety mechanisms relevant to campus-based platforms.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: VR gaming environments can engage citizens in smart city design by allowing them to experience and provide feedback on future urban mobility and infrastructure proposals.
methodology: Development of a VR gaming environment for a real-world future city project (Istanbul, Turkey) enabling citizen immersion and crowdsourced design feedback across four circulation modes.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer coordination platforms, identity-verified carpooling, or grassroots student-organized multi-module services in university settings.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: The Technology Acceptance Model explains primary school pupils' adoption of the ClassCraft gamification platform for sustainable mobility education.
methodology: Mixed methods: quantitative questionnaire based on the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) with 75 pupils aged 10–11, combined with qualitative Focus Group of 6 students.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address adult or university-student populations, real-world transportation coordination, identity verification, or multi-module campus platform design.
dataset_or_benchmark: Questionnaire data from 75 primary school pupils
theoretical_framework: Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)

key_claim: Evolutionary game theory can model how platform promotion policies perpetuate or counteract rating-based discrimination against marginalised service providers.
methodology: Evolutionary game theoretical model analyzing platform decisions to promote service providers based on reputation or protected-group membership.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots, student-organized coordination platforms or identity-verified community settings where users have repeated interactions rather than one-off transactions.
theoretical_framework: Evolutionary game theory applied to biased reputation systems on digital platforms

key_claim: Algorithmic management creates a gray zone blurring employee-freelancer distinctions, allowing platforms to circumvent traditional employment regulations.
methodology: Comprehensive literature review synthesizing research from HRM, organizational psychology, and organizational behavior on algorithmic management.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address non-commercial, community-driven platforms where users self-organize coordination without profit-maximizing algorithmic control.
theoretical_framework: Algorithmic management as a holistic system within HRM/OP/OB frameworks

key_claim: No significant gender differences exist in answer quality on Stack Overflow, yet gender disparities persist in community evaluation mechanisms.
methodology: Combined human evaluations and LLM-based automated assessments to measure answer quality across genders, examining interaction between gender, quality, and best-answer selection.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address rating fairness in identity-verified platforms or how gamification and verification mechanisms might mitigate gender bias in community ratings.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Mutual escrow, bidirectional dynamic rating, and a cooldown period reduce rating manipulation and retaliatory feedback in decentralized ride-sharing platforms.
methodology: Blockchain-based system design incorporating mutual escrow with default ratings for non-submitters, bidirectional dynamic reputation scoring based on historical consistency, and a cooldown period delaying rating submissions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification tied to institutional credentials, multi-module platform coordination beyond carpooling, or the specific trust dynamics of small-town university communities.
theoretical_framework: Blockchain-based mutual escrow and dynamic reputation framework

key_claim: DSE platforms embed design decisions that allow certain demographics to gain unfair advantage, identifiable through systematic synthesis of 58 interdisciplinary studies.
methodology: Systematic literature review synthesizing 58 interdisciplinary studies on discrimination in digital sharing economy platforms
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots user-driven coordination or identity-verification mechanisms as anti-discrimination design primitives in small-town university contexts
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Algorithmic management practices negatively influence workplace well-being both directly and indirectly through reduced job autonomy.
methodology: Conceptual model testing relationships between algorithmic management practices, job autonomy, total rewards, and workplace well-being using empirical data from European organisations
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how algorithmic management dynamics manifest in peer-to-peer multi-module platforms or non-employment contexts such as student-organized carpooling
theoretical_framework: Conceptual model linking algorithmic management practices to job autonomy, total rewards, and workplace well-being

key_claim: The 20-item Algorithmic Management Questionnaire validly measures workers' perceived exposure to algorithmic management across five dimensions and negatively relates to job autonomy and work engagement.
methodology: Six-phase scale development process with content, factorial, discriminant, convergent, and predictive validity testing across three samples of gig workers (n=1332)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address algorithmic management perceptions in non-gig, grassroots peer-to-peer platforms or contexts where users themselves design and govern the platform
dataset_or_benchmark: Three samples of gig workers (overall n=1332)
theoretical_framework: Higher-order construct model of algorithmic management comprising monitoring, goal setting, scheduling, performance rating, and compensation dimensions

key_claim: Women crowdworkers develop coping tactics to reduce gendered work–family conflicts, but digital labor platforms do not inherently challenge gendered labor market inequalities.
methodology: Ethnographic research with women platform workers in the UK using five crowdwork platforms (PeoplePerHour, Upwork, Freelancer, Fiverr, Copify)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address platform-mediated harm or safety concerns in physical service platforms like carpooling, nor coordination challenges in small-town university settings
dataset_or_benchmark: Ethnographic data from UK women crowdworkers on PeoplePerHour, Upwork, Freelancer, Fiverr, Copify
theoretical_framework: Work–family conflict theory applied to digital labor platforms and gendered employment

key_claim: Work design theory must expand beyond twentieth-century models to address algorithmic management, gig work, and lone work created by technological, demographic, and environmental changes.
methodology: Narrative literature review of work design research examining contextual, economic, technological, and diversity issues
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how grassroots, student-initiated platforms in small-town settings coordinate multi-domain needs outside traditional employer-employee work design frameworks.
theoretical_framework: Work design theory (job characteristics and related models)

key_claim: Independent platform workers can experience perceived organizational support, and the distributive fairness of managerial algorithms critically influences their job satisfaction.
methodology: Survey of 435 Uber drivers analyzed through the lens of Organizational Justice Theory examining procedural and distributive fairness perceptions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address fairness perceptions in peer-to-peer or identity-verified community platforms where algorithmic management is minimal and coordination is grassroots-driven.
theoretical_framework: Theory of Organizational Justice

key_claim: Integrating Mobility-as-a-Feature with the 15-minute city concept into local super apps can drive equitable and sustainable urban transitions with seamless service access.
methodology: Perspective paper combining literature review, practical application analysis, and a user survey (N=1,019)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how local super apps would function in small-town or university-campus contexts where population density and service availability differ fundamentally from urban 15-minute city models.
theoretical_framework: Mobility-as-a-Feature (MaaF) integrated with 15-minute city (15mC) concept

key_claim: Public perception of the PUSAKA super app for digitized Ministry of Religious Affairs services is generally positive but hampered by server and network errors.
methodology: Quantitative survey of PUSAKA users collecting data on user experience with digitized government services
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address multi-module coordination across diverse community needs (e.g., transportation, housing) or design primitives like identity verification and safety relevant to peer-to-peer campus platforms.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: SANTER can serve as an inclusive super-app supporting Samarinda's smart city transformation by bridging societal, business, environmental, and governmental digital governance gaps.
methodology: Qualitative descriptive approach using in-depth stakeholder interviews, observations, and document analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, safety mechanisms, or multi-module coordination challenges faced by specific user populations such as international students in small-town contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Stakeholder interviews with city officials, SANTER administrators, and citizens
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: SANTER can serve as an inclusive super-app supporting Samarinda's smart city transformation by bridging societal, business, environmental, and governmental digital governance gaps.
methodology: Qualitative descriptive approach using in-depth stakeholder interviews, observations, and document analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, safety mechanisms, or multi-module coordination challenges faced by specific user populations such as international students in small-town contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Stakeholder interviews with city officials, SANTER administrators, and citizens
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: SANTER can serve as an inclusive super-app supporting Samarinda's smart city transformation by bridging societal, business, environmental, and governmental digital governance gaps.
methodology: Qualitative descriptive approach using in-depth stakeholder interviews, observations, and document analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, safety mechanisms, or multi-module coordination challenges faced by specific user populations such as international students in small-town contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Stakeholder interviews with city officials, SANTER administrators, and citizens
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: An AI-powered placement app integrating ATS-ready resume building, practice modules, mock interviews, and personalized analytics outperforms traditional placement systems in preparing students for recruitment.
methodology: System design and conceptual development of an AI-powered student placement application with multiple integrated modules
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots peer-to-peer coordination needs, identity verification for safety, or multi-module platforms serving non-career domains such as transportation or housing.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: The Polri Super App Presisi application has been implemented appropriately and effectively for online SKCK issuance at Polres Solok Selatan.
methodology: Qualitative approach using interviews, observations, and documentation with 10 purposively sampled informants, analyzed via Sugiyono's data reduction method.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification or safety mechanisms in government super-apps compare to grassroots or peer-to-peer multi-module platforms.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: Higher education and longer work experience among gig workers are associated with heightened concerns about algorithmic fairness, job stability, and support mechanisms.
methodology: Survey of 102 gig workers in Chennai analyzed with non-parametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis and Chi-square) across five perception dimensions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how platform algorithmic control affects international students or identity-verified community platforms in small-town university settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: Primary survey data from 102 gig workers in Chennai
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: The temporariness of Working Holiday Visas fosters worker subjectivities that make precarious platform work acceptable for highly educated young migrants.
methodology: Biographical interviews tracing migration trajectories of Chilean and Argentinian gig workers on Working Holiday Visas in Germany, analyzed through a temporalities lens.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how international students in small-town university settings self-organize grassroots coordination platforms beyond gig work or how identity verification could mitigate precarity.
theoretical_framework: Temporalities and becoming framework applied to migration and platform work subjectivities

key_claim: Uganda's existing employment and digital platform laws are structurally inadequate to regulate gig economy worker classification, social protection, and taxation.
methodology: Critical literature review using case studies from ride-hailing, digital freelancing, and delivery platforms in Uganda, applying labour law, platform governance, and regulatory compliance frameworks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots platform design primitives such as identity verification, gamification, or community-driven safety mechanisms that could complement formal regulation.
theoretical_framework: Labour law theory, platform governance theory, and regulatory compliance frameworks

key_claim: Occupational context structures freelancers' capacity to interpret and navigate platform management, producing distinct experiences across self-presentation, flexibility, skilling, and sustainability.
methodology: Longitudinal qualitative analysis of 108 online freelancers across five occupational categories
what_it_does_NOT_address: How occupational diversity manifests in non-freelance, peer-to-peer coordination platforms such as grassroots carpooling in small-town university settings
dataset_or_benchmark: Longitudinal dataset of 108 online freelancers across five occupational categories
theoretical_framework: Occupational embeddedness framework for platform labor

key_claim: Technical opacity and power asymmetry in algorithmic management drive food-delivery gig workers to engage in sensemaking to understand and resist algorithmic control.
methodology: Qualitative sensemaking study of food-delivery platform workers navigating algorithmic management
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether identity-verified, user-governed platforms can mitigate the opacity and power asymmetry that centralized algorithmic management creates
theoretical_framework: Sensemaking theory applied to algorithmic management

key_claim: Amazon fulfillment center workers develop nuanced resistance practices against labor-tracking algorithmic systems, extending CSCW understanding of algorithmic management beyond gig work.
methodology: Two-year ethnographic research in Amazon fulfillment centers
what_it_does_NOT_address: How algorithmic management and worker resistance dynamics differ in peer-organized, non-corporate multi-module platforms serving small-town university communities
dataset_or_benchmark: Two-year ethnographic data from Amazon fulfillment centers
theoretical_framework: Burawoy's labor process theory (work games) applied to algorithmic control

key_claim: Gig economy has its own inherent logic as a stage in the evolution of employee-employer relations from industrial to digital era, but has significantly reduced work quality.
methodology: Systematic literature review of driving forces behind gig economy emergence and development prospects
what_it_does_NOT_address: How grassroots, identity-verified coordination platforms in small-town settings differ from commercial gig economy platforms in work quality and user welfare
dataset_or_benchmark: Current world literature on gig economy
theoretical_framework: Evolutionary framework of employee-employer relations from industrial to platform-mediated stages

key_claim: Algorithmic control shapes gig workers' engagement through psychological contracts, with standardized guidance fostering relational contracts while tracking evaluation and behavioral constraints undermine them.
methodology: Three-wave survey of 328 gig workers in China analyzing algorithmic control dimensions' effects on psychological contracts and engagement
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how algorithmic control mechanisms operate in non-commercial grassroots platforms where users self-organize coordination rather than being managed by a corporate platform.
dataset_or_benchmark: Three-wave survey of 328 Chinese gig workers
theoretical_framework: Psychological contract theory combined with perceived algorithmic fairness as moderator

key_claim: Different dimensions of algorithmic control have divergent effects on food delivery riders' mental health and risky riding behavior, mediated by work pressure and moderated by perceived autonomy.
methodology: Structural equation modeling and bootstrap analysis of survey data from 466 Chinese food delivery riders, using the Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) model
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address algorithmic control in peer-to-peer or community-driven platforms where safety and coordination are user-managed rather than imposed by corporate algorithms.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 466 Chinese food delivery riders
theoretical_framework: Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) model

key_claim: Four algorithmic control mechanisms—GPS tracking, rating systems, dynamic pricing, and automated sanctions—create distinct psychological risk pathways for public transport drivers.
methodology: PRISMA-guided systematic integrative review of 48 peer-reviewed studies (2016–2025) from Scopus, Web of Science, and PubMed with thematic synthesis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity-verified community platforms might mitigate algorithmic psychological harms through trust-based design primitives like rating fairness and gamification.
dataset_or_benchmark: 48 peer-reviewed studies from 4,812 initial records (Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed)
theoretical_framework: Integrated multilevel framework synthesizing Job Demand-Control Model, Conservation of Resources Theory, and Algorithmic Management Theory

key_claim: Gig workers experience compounding exploitation, insecurity, and exclusion from social security due to informal employment status and socio-economic positioning, prompting organized resistance.
methodology: Qualitative examination of gig workers' experiences of exploitation, insecurity, and contemporary resistance movements
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots platform design by student communities could address precarity-adjacent coordination gaps such as transportation insecurity in small-town university settings.
theoretical_framework: none

key_claim: App-based gig work operates through 'algorithmic HRM control,' an omnipresent system distinguished by pervasive algorithmic technologies and substantial influence of non-organizational parties over workers.
methodology: Qualitative research design examining app-workers' experiences of algorithmic control in gig economy platforms
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots worker self-organization or cooperative platform alternatives could counteract algorithmic HRM control in small-town or university settings
theoretical_framework: Algorithmic HRM control framework (extending traditional HRM control theory to algorithmic gig work)

key_claim: Labor process theory reveals how algorithmic management in platform-mediated gig work reproduces deskilling, exploitation, and complex control-resistance dynamics through automated task assignment, surveillance, and performance evaluation.
methodology: Theoretical analysis applying labor process theory (LPT) to algorithmic management in platform-mediated gig work
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity-verified community platforms or user-driven coordination designs that could mitigate algorithmic exploitation in localized contexts such as university towns
theoretical_framework: Labor Process Theory (LPT)
